title: EXPOSÉ I SUMMARY OF THE FIRST EXPOSÉS OF A. GROTHENDIECK

pages.title: EXPOSÉ I SUMMARY OF THE FIRST EXPOSÉS OF A. GROTHENDIECK

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ I
SUMMARY OF THE FIRST EXPOSÉS OF A. GROTHENDIECKwritten up by P. Deligne
0. Preliminaries
0.0. Terminology
Let us recall the following definitions:
(0.0.1) trait: the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring. We often denote by \(\eta\) and \(s\) the generic and closed points of a trait.
(0.0.2) strictly local: for a ring: local henselian with separably closed residue field. For a scheme: the spectrum of such a ring.

(0.0.3) geometric point of \(S\) (in this exposé): a morphism \(\bar{x}\colon \operatorname{Spec}(\bar{k})\to S\) with image \(x\in S\), \(k(\bar{x})=_{\mathrm{dfn}}\bar{k}\) being a separable closure of \(k(x)\). For a henselian trait \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\), we often denote by \(\bar\eta\) a generic geometric point (i.e. with image \(\eta\)) of \(S\). The residue field of the valuation ring \(\bar V\), the integral closure of \(V\) in \(k(\bar\eta)\), is a separably closed algebraic extension of \(k(s)\) and defines a geometric point \(\bar s\) of \(S\) localized at \(s\).
(0.0.4) strict localization of \(X\) at the geometric point \(\bar x\): SGA 4 VIII 4.4.

(0.0.5) essentially of finite type over \(S\): for a local \(S\)-scheme (resp. local henselian, resp. strictly local): the spectrum of a local ring (resp. of the henselization of a local ring, resp. of a strict localization) of a scheme of finite type over \(S\).
(0.0.6) \(F\longmapsto F(n)\): Tate twist.
(0.0.7) An endomorphism \(T\) of a finite-dimensional vector space is quasi-unipotent if its eigenvalues are roots of unity, i.e. if there exist \(N\geq1\), \(M\geq1\) such that \((T^N-1)^M=0\).
0.1. Review of \(\ell\)-adic cohomology
Let \(X\) be a scheme of finite type over an algebraically closed field \(k\) of characteristic exponent \(p\) and \(\ell\) a prime number prime to \(p\).
The \(\ell\)-adic cohomology groups of \(X\) were defined in SGA 4 and SGA 5.

1. a) \(H^i(X,\mathbb Z/\ell^n)\) is the \(i\)-th cohomology group of the étale site \(X_{\mathrm{et}}\) of \(X\) with values in \(\mathbb Z/\ell^n\);
  2. b) by definition, \(H^i(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)=\varprojlim_n H^i(X,\mathbb Z/\ell^n)\);
  3. c) by definition, \(H^i(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)=H^i(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell\).
The \(\ell\)-adic cohomology groups with proper supports of \(X\) are defined similarly, starting from the \(H_c^i(X,\mathbb Z/\ell^n)\) of SGA 4 XVII.
In each of the following cases, one can prove that these groups \(H^i(X)\) have reasonable finiteness properties:
(0.1.1) in cohomology with proper support;
(0.1.2) for \(X\) proper over \(k\);
(0.1.3) for \(X\) smooth over \(k\) (thanks to a) and to Poincaré duality SGA 4 XVIII);
(0.1.4) when resolution of singularities à la Hironaka is available for schemes of finite type over \(k\), of dimension \(\leq\dim(X)\);

In all these cases, the \(H^i(X,\mathbb Z/\ell^n)\) are finite, the \(H^i(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\) of finite type, the \(H^i(X,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) finite-dimensional and (except perhaps for (0.1.5)) we have split short exact sequences \[0\longrightarrow H^i(X,\mathbb Z_\ell)\otimes\mathbb Z/\ell^n \longrightarrow H^i(X,\mathbb Z/\ell^n)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Tor}_1(H^{i+1}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell),\mathbb Z/\ell^n)\longrightarrow0.\]

Moreover, the proof of the finiteness theorem provides in each case a proof of a generic constructibility theorem: if \(k\) is the field of fractions of an integral noetherian scheme \(S\) and \(X\) is the generic fiber of \(f_1\colon X_1\to S\) (of finite type), there exists a nonempty open set \(U\subset S\) such that, over \(U\), the \(R^if_{1*}\mathbb Z/\ell^n\) are locally constant, with formation compatible with any base change.

0.2. Let us no longer assume \(k\) algebraically closed, and let \(\bar k\) be an algebraic (or separable, which would come to the same thing) closure of \(k\). We shall consider the "geometric" cohomology groups \(H^i(X_{\bar k})\), where \(X_{\bar k}\) is the scheme deduced from \(X\) by extension of scalars from \(k\) to \(\bar k\). By transport of structure, the Galois group \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar k/k)\) acts on these groups. In \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-cohomology, when we are in one of the cases (0.1.1) to (0.1.5), we thus obtain continuous representations of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar k/k)\) in a linear group \(\operatorname{GL}(n,\mathbb Q_\ell)\).
0.3. Review of traits (0.0.1)

For the proofs, we refer to [4]. Let \(S\) be a henselian trait, with \(\eta,s,V,\bar V,\bar\eta,\bar s\) as in (0.0.1) (0.0.3). We denote by \(p\) the characteristic exponent of \(k(s)\) and by \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar s/s)\)) the Galois group \(\operatorname{Gal}(k(\bar\eta)/k(\eta))\) (resp. ...). By transport of structure, \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) acts on \(k(\bar s)\), whence a map from \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) to (in fact onto) \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar s/s)\), whose kernel is the inertia group \(I\). The subfield of \(k(\bar\eta)\) defined by \(I\) is a maximal unramified extension of \(k(\eta)\).

The profinite group \(I\) admits a largest subgroup \(P\) which is a pro-\(p\)-group. The quotient \(I/P\) is the tame inertia group. We have canonically \[I/P\simeq\varprojlim_n\mu_n(k(\bar s))=_{\mathrm{dfn}}\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s)).\] If \(\phi_n\) is the canonical map from \(I/P\) to \(\mu_n(\bar k)\), and \(u\) is an element of \(\bar V\) of valuation \(1/n\), we have for \(\sigma\in I\) \[\sigma(u)\equiv\phi_n(u)\cdot u\pmod{\text{elements of valuation }>1/n}.\]
In summary, we have:

(0.3.1) \[\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\supset I\supset P,\qquad \operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)/I\simeq\operatorname{Gal}(\bar s/s),\qquad I/P\simeq\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s)),\] \[P:\text{ pro-}p\text{-group }(\{e\}\text{ if }p=1),\] and the action (by inner automorphisms in \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\)) of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)/I\) on \(I/P\) is identified with the natural action of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar s/s)\) on \(\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s))\).

0.4. More generally, let \(S\) be strictly local regular, \(D\) a regular divisor in \(S\) and \(p'\) the characteristic exponent of the generic point of \(D\). Let \(\bar\eta\) be a generic geometric point of \(S\) and \(\bar s\) the geometric point localized at the closed point \(s\) which is deduced from it. By Abhyankar's lemma, the fundamental group \(\pi_1(S-D,\bar\eta)\) admits a dévissage analogous to (0.3.1):

(0.4.1) \[\pi_1(S-D,\bar\eta)\supset I\supset P,\qquad \pi_1(S-D,\bar\eta)/I=\operatorname{Gal}(\bar s/s),\qquad I/P\simeq\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s)),\] \[P:\text{ with no quotient of order prime to }p'.\] The action by inner automorphisms of \(\pi_1(S-D,\bar\eta)/I\) on \(I/P\) is identified with the natural action of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar s/s)\) on \(\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s))\).
0.5. Néron desingularization
Néron's desingularization method provides the following result:

blocks.title: Lemma 0.5.1.
blocks.html:
Lemma 0.5.1.

Let \(S\to S_0\) be a morphism of henselian traits, with \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\), \(S_0=\operatorname{Spec}(V_0)\), and \(\pi\) a uniformizer of \(V_0\). We assume that \(\pi\) is a uniformizer of \(V\), and that the extensions \(k(s)/k(s_0)\) and \(k(\eta)/k(\eta_0)\) are separable. Then \(V\) is an inductive limit of \(V_0\)-algebras \(B_i\) which are henselian, smooth and essentially of finite type over \(V_0\).
To construct the desired \(V_0\)-algebras, one takes \(V_0\subset B\subset V\) with \(B\) of finite type, and desingularizes \(\operatorname{Spec}(B)\) à la Néron ([1] § 4).
Let \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\) be a henselian trait.

(0.5.2) If \(S\) is of equal characteristic, with uniformizer \(\pi\), we can apply (0.5.1) with \(S_0=\mathbb F_p[\pi]_{(\pi)}\). The extension \(s/s_0\) is separable since \(\mathbb F_p\) is perfect; the extension \(\eta/\eta_0\) is separable since \(\pi\), being a uniformizer, is not a \(p\)-th power (if \(p\ne1\)) and hence forms part of a \(p\)-basis. We find that \(S\) is a limit of spectra of smooth henselian \(\mathbb F_p[\pi]_{(\pi)}\)-algebras essentially of finite type.

(0.5.3) If \(S\) is complete, of unequal characteristic, with perfect residue field \(k\), then \(V\) is an Eisenstein extension of the ring of Witt vectors (= Cohen ring) \(W(k)\) \[V\simeq W(k)[\pi]/\left(\pi^n+\sum_0^{n-1}a_i\pi^i\right).\] If we perturb the \(a_i\) slightly, we obtain an isomorphic extension. We may therefore assume that the \(a_i\) lie in \(W(k')\), with \(k'\) of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\). Let \(k_0\) be the perfect closure of \(k'\) in \(k\) and \[V_0=W(k_0)[\pi]/\left(\pi^n+\sum_0^{n-1}a_i\pi^i\right).\] Lemma 0.5.1 applies to \(V/V_0\), and the residue field of \(V_0\) is a radicial extension of a field of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\).

pages.title: 1. Arithmetic proof of the monodromy theorem

blocks.html: 1. Arithmetic proof of the monodromy theorem
The reader will find in [5], Appendix, a proof of the following result of A. Grothendieck.

blocks.title: Proposition 1.1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1.

Let \(S\) be a henselian trait, \(\ell\) a prime number prime to the characteristic exponent \(p\) of \(k(s)\) and \(\rho\) a continuous representation of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) in \(\operatorname{GL}(n,\mathbb Q_\ell)\). We assume that the following condition is satisfied:

\((\star_\ell)\) No finite extension of \(k(s)\) contains all the roots of unity of order a power of \(\ell\).

Then there exists an open subgroup \(I_1\) of the inertia group \(I\) such that \(\rho(\sigma)\) is unipotent for \(\sigma\in I_1\).
The condition \((\star_\ell)\) is automatically satisfied for \(k(s)\) of finite type over the prime field (or radicial over such a field).
The following theorem follows immediately from 1.1.

blocks.title: Monodromy Theorem 1.2.
blocks.html:
Monodromy Theorem 1.2.

With the preceding notation, let \(X\) be a scheme of finite type over \(\eta\) and \(H\) a cohomology space of \(X_{\bar\eta}\), with coefficients in \(\mathbb Q_\ell\), of one of the types (1.1.1) to (1.1.5). If condition \((\star_\ell)\) is satisfied, the action of any element of \(I\) on \(H\) is quasi-unipotent.
The passage-to-the-limit results of 0.5 allow us to dispose of the hypothesis \((\star_\ell)\).

blocks.title: Variant 1.3.
blocks.html:
Variant 1.3.

The condition \((\star_\ell)\) in 1.2 is superfluous.

Let \(H'_0\) be a \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-cohomology space of \(X_{\bar\eta}\) which gives rise to \(H\), and \(H_0=H'_0/\text{torsion}\). By hypothesis we have \(H=H_0\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell\), and the action of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) is deduced from \[\rho\colon\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\longrightarrow\operatorname{GL}(H_0).\] Let \(I_{\ell'}\) be the largest subgroup prime to \(\ell\) of \(I\). Its image in \(\operatorname{GL}(H_0)\) is an \(\ell\)-adic Lie group prime to \(\ell\), hence is finite.

Let us perform an extension of traits \(S'\to S\). This does not change \(H_0\) (SGA 4 XVI 6.6), and \(\rho(I')\) is of finite index in \(\rho(I)\), since \(I'/P'\) is of finite index in \(I/P\), by what precedes. It therefore suffices to prove the theorem over \(S'\): we may assume that

1. a) \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) acts trivially on \(H_0/\ell H_0\);
  2. b) if \(S\) is of unequal characteristic, \(S\) is complete with perfect residue field (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 10.3.1).

Set \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\), define \(V_0\) as in (0.5.2) and (0.5.3) and apply (0.5.1). We have \(V=\varinjlim B_i\), with \(S_i=\operatorname{Spec}(B_i)\) essentially smooth of finite type over \(V_0\). Let \(D_i\subset S_i\) have equation \(\pi=0\). For \(i\) large enough, \(H_0\) comes from a twisted constant \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf \(\mathcal H_i\) on \(S_i-D_i\) (by "generic constructibility" (0.1)) and \(\mathcal H_i/\ell\mathcal H_i\) is constant.
The dévissages (0.3.1) and (0.4.1) give rise to commutative diagrams:

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2em{ {\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\supset I\supset P} \ar[d]& I/P\ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[d]&{\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)}\ar@{=}[d]\\ {\pi_1(S_i-D_i,\bar\eta)\supset I_i\supset P_i}& I_i/P_i\ar[r]^{\sim}&{\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)} }\tag{1.3.1}\] and the representation \(H_0\) of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) is deduced from an action of \(\pi_1(S_i-D_i,\bar\eta)\) on \(H_0\), trivial on \(H_0/\ell H_0\). The group \(\operatorname{Ker}(\operatorname{GL}(H)\to\operatorname{GL}(H/\ell H))\) is a pro-\(\ell\)-group; \(P_i\) therefore acts trivially, and we apply the following variant of 1.1.

blocks.title: Variant 1.4.
blocks.html:
Variant 1.4.

Let us take up the notation of 0.4 again and let \(\rho\) be a continuous representation of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) in \(\operatorname{GL}(n,\mathbb Q_\ell)\). We assume that \(k(s)\) satisfies \((\star_\ell)\) and that \(\rho(P)\) is finite. Then the action of \(I\) is quasi-unipotent.
2. Vanishing cycles

2.1. Let \(S\) be a strictly local trait, and let us take up the notation of 0.3 again. By hypothesis, we have here \(s=\bar s\). Let \(f\colon X\to S\) be a scheme of finite type over \(S\). The formalism of vanishing cycles is a tool for studying the relation between the cohomologies of \(X_s\) and of \(X_{\bar\eta}\), and the action of the inertia group on that of \(X_{\bar\eta}\), starting from local information (on \(X\)) about the morphism \(f\).
A key result of this type is the specialization theorem SGA 4 XVI 2.2, according to which for \(f\) proper and smooth, \(X_s\) and \(X_{\bar\eta}\) have the same cohomology.
In the transcendental theory, if \(f\colon X\to D\) is a proper morphism from an analytic space \(X\) to the disc \(D\), vanishing cycles appear as follows:

1. a) After shrinking \(D\) if necessary, we may assume that \(X\) is a topological fiber bundle over the punctured disc \(D^*\) (Thom's isotopy theorem). In particular, the fibers \(X_t\) (\(t\in D^*\)) are all isomorphic.
  2. b) It is not unreasonable to picture the special fiber \(X_s\) as deduced from "the" general fiber \(X_t\) by contracting polyhedra in \(X_t\): we would have \(\pi\colon X_t\to X_s\). The method of vanishing cycles then consists in studying the difference between the cohomologies of \(X_s\) and \(X_t\) by means of the Leray spectral sequence of \(\pi\).

The geometric theory explained below is very close to this transcendental theory; one must replace the general point \(t\in D\) by \(\bar\eta\), the generic geometric point of \(S\). Conversely, one can model the transcendental theory on the geometric theory, replacing \(t\) or \(\bar\eta\) by the universal covering \(\widetilde D^*\) of \(D^*\), and \(X_t\) or \(X_{\bar\eta}\) by \(X\times_D\widetilde D^*\). In doing so, one gets rid of many \(\varepsilon\) and \(\eta\), but sometimes loses information.
2.2. Let us fix a torsion ring \(\Lambda\) in which \(p\) is invertible (for example \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z/\ell^n\mathbb Z\), \(\ell\) prime to \(p\)). Consider the morphisms:
\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ X_s\ar[r]^i\ar[d]_{f_s}&X\ar[d]^f&X_{\bar\eta}\ar[l]_{\bar j}\ar[d]^{f_{\bar\eta}}\\ s\ar[r]&S&\bar\eta\ar[l] }\]
The sheaves of vanishing cycles \(\psi^i\) on \(X_s\) are the
\[\psi^i=i^*R^i\bar j_*\Lambda.\tag{2.2.1}\]
If \(f\) is proper, we have (SGA 4 XII 5.5)
\[i^*\colon H^p(X,R^q\bar j_*\Lambda)\xrightarrow{\sim} H^p(X_s,i^*R^q\bar j_*\Lambda),\tag{2.2.2}\]
and the Leray spectral sequence for \(\bar j\) can be rewritten
\[H^p(X_s,\psi^q)\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda).\tag{2.2.3}\]

When nothing happens at infinity, one can sometimes obtain this spectral sequence without any properness hypothesis. That some hypothesis at infinity is needed can already be seen in the trivial case \(X_s=\varnothing\).
We deduce immediately from the definitions:

blocks.title: Proposition 2.3.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3.

Let \(\bar x\) be a geometric point of \(X_s\) (for example a closed point), \(X_{(\bar x)}\) the strict henselization of \(X\) at \(\bar x\) and \(X_{(\bar x)\bar\eta}\) the generic geometric fiber of the projection of \(X_{(\bar x)}\) onto \(S\). We have \[\psi^i_{\bar x}=H^i(X_{(\bar x)\bar\eta},\Lambda).\]

blocks.title: Corollary 2.4.
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4.

Where \(f\) is smooth, \(\psi^i=0\) for \(i>0\) and \(\psi^0=\Lambda\).
This is SGA 4 XV 2.1.
The group \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) (equal by hypothesis to the inertia group) acts by transport of structure on the \(\psi^i\), and

blocks.title: Scholium 2.5.
blocks.html:
Scholium 2.5.

The spectral sequence 2.2.3 is \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\)-equivariant.

blocks.title: Variant 2.6.
blocks.html:
Variant 2.6.

Let \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\) and \(\bar X=X\times_S\bar S\). \(X_{\bar\eta}\) is the complement of \(X_s=X_{\bar s}\) in \(\bar X\). Set \[\phi^i=H^{i+1}_{X_s}(\bar X,\Lambda)\] (cohomology sheaves with support). We have an exact sequence of sheaves \[0\longrightarrow\phi^{-1}\longrightarrow\Lambda\longrightarrow\psi^0 \longrightarrow\phi^0\longrightarrow0\] and, for \(i>0\), isomorphisms \(\psi^i\xrightarrow{\sim}\phi^i\). For \(f\) smooth, the \(\phi^i\) are zero.

Set \(\phi^i_{\mathrm{gl}}=H^i_{X_s}(\bar X,\Lambda)\) (global analogue of the \(\phi^i\)). We have a spectral sequence \[H^p(X_s,\phi^q)\Longrightarrow\phi^{p+q}_{\mathrm{gl}}\tag{2.6.1}\] and a long exact cohomology sequence which, for \(f\) proper, can be rewritten \[\cdots\longrightarrow H^i(X_s,\Lambda)\longrightarrow H^i(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda) \longrightarrow\phi^i_{\mathrm{gl}}\longrightarrow\cdots.\tag{2.6.2}\]

blocks.title: Variant 2.7.
blocks.html:
Variant 2.7.

Let \(K_t\) be the maximal tamely ramified extension of \(K\) in \(\bar K\). We set \(X_t=X\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(K_t)\). Let \(\bar j_t\) be the projection of \(X_t\) into \(X\). We define tame ("tame") variants of the \(\psi^i\) by setting \[\psi_t^i=R^i\bar j_{t*}\Lambda.\] Using the fact that \(P\) is a pro-\(p\)-group, with \(p\) invertible in \(\Lambda\), we find that \[H^*(X_t,\Lambda)=H^*(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda)^P,\tag{2.7.1}\] \[\psi_t^i=\psi^{iP}.\tag{2.7.2}\] For \(f\) proper, the Leray spectral sequence of \(\bar j_t\) is deduced from 2.2.3 by passage to the \(P\)-invariants: \[H^p(X_s,\psi_t^q)\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(X_t,\Lambda)=H^{p+q}(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda)^P.\tag{2.7.3}\]

pages.title: 3. Geometric proof of the monodromy theorem

blocks.html: 3. Geometric proof of the monodromy theorem
Let \(\Lambda\) be as in 2.2.

blocks.title: Conjecture 3.1 (purity).
blocks.html:
Conjecture 3.1 (purity).

Let \(A\) be a strictly local excellent (0.2) regular ring and \(D\) a regular divisor of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) defined by a parameter \(x\). Let \(U=\operatorname{Spec}(A)-D=\operatorname{Spec}(A[1/x])\). We have \[H^q(U,\Lambda)=\begin{cases} \Lambda&\text{for }q=0,\\ \Lambda(-1)&\text{for }q=1\quad\text{(notation (0.0.6))},\\ 0&\text{for }q>1. \end{cases}\tag{3.1.1}\]

Here are cases where this conjecture is known (SGA 4 XIX): for \(q=0\) or \(1\), for \(A\) of characteristic 0, for \(A\) of equal characteristic \(p\) when resolution of singularities is available in dimension \(\leq\dim(A)\), and in characteristic \(p\), or for \((\operatorname{Spec}(A),D)\) a smooth pair (relative purity theorem SGA 4 XVI 3.7).

When purity is available, and \(D=\sum_{i\in B}D_i\) is a divisor with normal crossings in \(\operatorname{Spec}A\), defined by part of a regular system of parameters, the cohomology of \(U=\operatorname{Spec}(A)-D\) is given by
\[H^0(U,\Lambda)=\Lambda,\qquad H^1(U,\Lambda)=\Lambda(-1)^B,\qquad H^q(U,\Lambda)=\bigwedge^q H^1(U,\Lambda)\quad\text{(given by the cup-product).}\tag{3.1.2}\]
These formulas are identical to those which, over \(\mathbb C\), give the cohomology of \(D^{*B}\times D^k\).

3.2. Let \(S\) be a strictly local trait. We keep the notations (0.0.1) (0.0.3). Let \(f\colon X\to S\) be a morphism of finite type. We assume that \(X\) is regular, that \(X_\eta\) is smooth and that \(X_s\) is a divisor with normal crossings in \(X\). We will assume that this divisor is (globally, and not only locally for the étale topology) a sum of regular divisors: \((X_s)_{\mathrm{red}}=\sum_{i\in B}D_i\) (the general case can be recovered by localization). Denote by \(m_i\) their multiplicities: \(X_s=\sum_{i\in B}m_iD_i\).

Let \(x\) be a point of \(X_s\), \(\bar x\) a geometric point of \(X\) localized at \(x\) (for example \(x\) closed, \(x=\bar x\)), \(C=\{j\mid x\in D_j\}\), and \(K\) the complex concentrated in degrees 0 and \(-1\) \[K:\mathbb Z^C\xrightarrow d\mathbb Z,\qquad d((n_j))=\sum_jm_jn_j.\]

blocks.title: Theorem 3.3 (uses purity).
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.3 (uses purity).

The groups of tame vanishing cycles \(\psi^i_{t\bar x}\) are the following:

(i) We have a canonical isomorphism of graded \(\Lambda\)-algebras \[\psi^*_{t\bar x}\simeq\bigwedge^*\bigl(H_1(K)\otimes\Lambda(-1)\bigr) \otimes_\Lambda\psi^0_{t\bar x}.\]

(ii) \(\psi^0_{t\bar x}\) is a \(\Lambda\)-algebra \(\Lambda^E\), for \(E\) a set on which the tame inertia group \(I_t=I/P=\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s))\) acts transitively, whose number of elements is the largest divisor prime to \(p\) of \(\#H_0(K)\).
Here is the method of proof.

a) Let \(X_{(\bar x)}\) be the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\bar x\), \(U=X_{(\bar x)}-X_{(\bar x)s}\) (the complement of a divisor with normal crossings), \(t_j\) a local equation for \(D_j\), \(X_n=X_{(\bar x)}[(t_j^{1/n})]\) (for \((n,p)=1\); it is a regular scheme) and \(U_n\) the inverse image of \(U\) in \(X_n\). By 3.1.2, we have \(H^q(U_n,\Lambda)=\bigwedge^q(\Lambda(-1)^C)\); for \(m=nd\), the "inverse image" map from \(H^q(U_n,\Lambda)\) into \(H^q(U_m,\Lambda)\) is multiplication by \(d^q\). Setting \(\widetilde U=\varprojlim_{(n,p)=1}U_n\), we thus have \[H^0(\widetilde U,\Lambda)=\Lambda,\qquad H^q(\widetilde U,\Lambda)=\varinjlim_nH^q(U_n,\Lambda)=0\quad\text{for }q\ne0.\tag{3.3.1}\]

b) \(\widetilde U\) is a pro-covering of \(U\) with group \(\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s))^C\). By definition, we have \(\psi^*_{t\bar x}=H^*(U_{\eta_t},\Lambda)\); \(\widetilde U\) is a pro-covering with group \(H_1(K)\otimes\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s))\) of each of the connected components of \(U_{\eta_t}\), and these are permuted transitively by the tame inertia group. From (3.3.1) and the Hochschild-Serre spectral sequence, we then deduce the formulas 3.3.

In pictorial terms, we may say that \(U_{\bar\eta}\) is the fiber of a morphism \(\delta\) from a cohomological \(K(\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s))^C,1)\) \(U\) into the cohomological \(K(\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s)),1)\) \(\eta\), this morphism \(\delta\) inducing \(d=\sum m_jn_j\colon\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s))^C\to \widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)(k(\bar s))\).

blocks.title: Corollary 3.4 (uses purity).
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4 (uses purity).

Suppose \(f\) is proper. Let \(N\) be the least common multiple of the multiplicities \(m_j\) (\(j\in B\)). Let \(q>0\), and \(q'\) the infimum of \(q\) and of the maximum number of divisors \(D_j\) passing through a single point. For \(T\) in the tame inertia group \(I_t=I/P\), we have \[(T^N-1)^{q'}=0\quad\text{on }H^q(X_{\bar\eta},\Lambda)^P.\]
This follows immediately from 3.3 and from the variant 2.7 of 2.5.
The required purity results are available for the \(H^1\), in characteristic 0, or for \(S\) essentially of finite type over a field.

blocks.title: Theorem 3.5.
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.5.

Let \(C\) be a smooth projective curve over the field of fractions \(k(\eta)\) of a discrete valuation ring \(V\). The inertia group \(I\) acts on \(H^1(C_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\) by quasi-unipotent automorphisms of level 2: there exists \(N\) such that, for \(T\in I\), we have \[(T^N-1)^2=0\quad\text{on }H^1(C_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell).\]
We reduce to assuming \(V\) strictly local. The assertion is that there exists an open subgroup \(I_1\) of \(I\) such that \((T-1)^2=0\) on \(H^1\) for \(T\in I_1\).

We are free to extend the scalars from \(k(\eta)\) to a finite extension (replacing \(V\) by its normalization in this extension). Since the image of the pro-\(p\)-group \(P\) in an \(\ell\)-adic Lie group \(\operatorname{GL}(n,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is finite, we may in particular assume that \(P\) acts trivially.

Let \(C_0\) be a minimal model of \(C\) over \(V\) (for the existence of \(C_0\), based on Abhyankar's theorem, cf. the discussion in [3] § 2, p. 87; one could here reduce to the case \(V\) excellent by passing to the completion of \(V\): invoke SGA 4 XVI 1.6). Let \(C_1\) be a regular model of \(C\), deduced from \(C_0\) by blow-ups, whose special fiber is a divisor with normal crossings. The arguments 3.3, 3.4 apply to \(C_1\), \(\psi_t^0\), \(\psi_t^1\) and \(H^1(C_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Z/\ell^n)^P=H^1(C_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Z/\ell^n)\) with \(N\) independent of \(n\), and 3.5 follows.

Remark 3.6. Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over \(k(\eta)\). Since \(A\) is a quotient of a Jacobian, we find again that the action of \(I\) on \(H^1(A_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\) or \(T_\ell(A)\) is quasi-unipotent of level 2.
Remark 3.7. The action of \(I\) on \(H^1(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is in fact quasi-unipotent of level 2 for every scheme \(X\) of finite type over \(\eta\).
Theorem 3.5 (or 3.6) is at the basis of the proof of the stable reduction theorem for abelian varieties which will be given in Exposé IX § 3.

3.8. It is clear that when purity and resolution of singularities are available (for example in characteristic 0) the preceding method proves the monodromy theorem, and provides bounds for the exponent of nilpotence. Thus, in equal characteristic 0, we find that for \(X\) proper and smooth over \(\eta\) and \(T\) in an open subgroup of \(I\), we have \[(T-1)^{q+1}=0\quad\text{on }H^q(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Z_\ell) \qquad(T\in I_1\subset I).\]

pages.title: 4. Criteria for the vanishing of the sheaves of vanishing cycles

blocks.html: 4. Criteria for the vanishing of the sheaves of vanishing cycles
4.1. Let \(S\) be a strictly local trait and \(f\colon X\to S\) a scheme of finite type over \(S\). Set \(n=\dim(X_\eta)\).
If \(X'\) is the schematic closure of \(X_\eta\) in \(X\), we have:

1. a) on \(X_s-X'_s\), \(\phi^{-1}=\Lambda\) and the other \(\phi^i\) are zero;
  2. b) on \(X'_s\), the \(\phi^i\) and \(\psi^i\) relative to \(X/S\) or to \(X'/S\) coincide, and \(\phi^{-1}=0\).
Since \(X'\) is flat over \(S\), this often makes it possible to reduce to the case where \(X\) is flat over \(S\).

blocks.title: Theorem 4.2.
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.2.

(i) We have \(\psi^i=0\) for \(i>n\).

(ii) More precisely, for \(x\) a point of \(X_s\) whose closure is of dimension \(k\) (\(k=\deg\operatorname{tr}(k(x)/k(s))\)) and for \(\bar x\) a geometric point localized at \(x\), we have \[\psi^i_{\bar x}=0\quad\text{for }i>n-k,\quad\text{i.e.}\quad d(\psi^i)\leq n-i\quad\text{(notation of SGA 4 XIV 2.1).}\]

We have \(\psi^i_{\bar x}=H^i(X_{(\bar x)\bar\eta},\Lambda)\), and \(X_{(\bar x)\bar\eta}\) is a projective limit of affine schemes of dimension \(\leq n\) over \(k(\bar\eta)\). Assertion (i) therefore follows from the affine Lefschetz theorem (SGA 4 XIV 3.2) by passage to the limit.

To prove (ii), we may assume that \(X\) is flat over \(S\) (4.1) and that there exists a quasi-finite flat \(S\)-morphism \(g\colon X\to\mathbb A_S^k\) such that \(g(x)\) is the generic point of \(\mathbb A_s^k\). Let \(S'\) be the trait given by the strict localization of \(\mathbb A_S^k\) at \(\bar x\). We have:

1. a) \(S'_{\bar\eta}\) is the spectrum of a field, and \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta'/S'_{\bar\eta})\) is a \(p\)-group \(Q\).
  2. b) By (i) for \(g\), we have \(H^i(X_{(\bar x)\bar\eta'},\Lambda)=0\) for \(i>n-k\).
  3. c) We have \(H^i(X_{(\bar x)\bar\eta},\Lambda)=H^i(X_{(\bar x)\bar\eta'},\Lambda)^Q\), whence the theorem.

blocks.title: Corollary 4.3.
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3.

If \(f\) is proper and flat and the dimension of the non-smoothness locus of \(f\) in \(X_s\) is \(\leq d\), the specialization morphism \(H^i(X_s)\to H^i(X_{\bar\eta})\) is an isomorphism for \(i>n+d+1\) and an epimorphism for \(i=n+d+1\).

4.4. To prove, in certain cases, that the \(\phi^i\) for \(i\) small are zero, we will rely on the theory of local duality (directly or via the local Lefschetz theorems of SGA 2 XIV). This tool is available only in characteristic 0 (or in equal characteristic \(p\) when resolution of singularities à la Hironaka is available in the dimensions under consideration).

blocks.title: Theorem 4.5.
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.5.

Suppose \(S\) is of characteristic 0. Let \(f\colon X\to S\) be a morphism of finite type and \(x\) a closed point of \(X_s\). We assume that

\(a\) \(x\) is an isolated point of non-smoothness in its fiber;

\(b\) \(X\) is of relative geometric depth \(\geq n\) at \(x\): in a neighborhood of \(x\), \(X\) can be identified with a subscheme defined by \(k\) equations in a smooth scheme of relative dimension \(N\) at \(x\), with \(n\leq N-k\).

Then \(\phi^i_{\bar x}=0\) for \(i<n\).

Let \(X_x\) be the strict localization of \(X\) at \(x\) and \(V=X_x-\{x\}\). For every trait \(S'\) finite over \(S\), let likewise \(X'_x=X_x\times_SS'\) and \(V'=V\times_SS'\). For \(\bar S\) the normalization of \(S\) in \(\bar\eta\), let \(\bar X_x=X_x\times_S\bar S\), \(\bar V=V\times_S\bar S\). We have:

(1) If \(\phi^i=0\) on \((X_x)_s\) (i.e. at every generization of \(x\) in \(X_s\)) for \(i\leq m\), then \[H^{i-1}_{\{x\}}(\bar X_x)=\widetilde H^i(\bar V)\xrightarrow{\sim} \widetilde H^i(V_{\bar\eta})=\phi^i_x\quad\text{for }i\leq m.\] The \(\phi^i\) are indeed cohomology sheaves with support in \(V_s\) of \(\bar V\); under the hypotheses of 4.5, \(a\) is applicable for \(m=\infty\) (SGA 4 XV 2.1).

(2) The hypothesis \(b\) of 4.5 is stable under base change. By SGA 2 XIV 5.6, it implies that \[\widetilde H^i_{\{x\}}(V')=H^{i-1}_{X'_x}(X'_x)=0\quad\text{for }i<n;\] we conclude by noting that \(\widetilde H^i(\bar V)\) is an inductive limit of the \(\widetilde H^i(V')\).
By 4.2 and 4.5, we have:

blocks.title: Corollary 4.6.
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.

For \(S\) a henselian trait of characteristic 0, \(X/S\) a relative complete intersection of relative dimension \(n\) and \(x\in X_s\) an isolated point of non-smoothness in its fiber, we have \(\phi^i_x=0\) for \(i\ne n\).

blocks.title: Corollary 4.7.
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7.

Under the hypotheses of 4.6, \(\phi^n_x\) is a flat \(\Lambda\)-module.

For \(\Lambda'\) a finite \(\Lambda\)-algebra, general nonsense (SGA 4 XVIII 5.2.13) indeed provides, for the \(\phi^i_{\Lambda'}\) relative to \(\Lambda'\), \[\phi^{n-i}_{\Lambda'}=\operatorname{Tor}^{\Lambda}_i(\Lambda',\phi^n_\Lambda),\] and we apply 4.6 to \(\Lambda'\).

blocks.title: Variant 4.8.
blocks.html:
Variant 4.8.

In 4.5, let us drop the condition \(a\) and let \(Z\) be the non-smoothness locus of \(f\). We can still conclude \[\phi^i_{\bar x}=0\quad\text{for }i<n-\dim Z_s.\]
We proceed by descending induction on \(\dim\overline{\{x\}}\). The induction hypothesis allows us to apply 4.5 (1) with \(m=n-\dim Z_s\), and we complete the proof as before.
4.9. It is possible to obtain a vanishing criterion using only information on the geometric fibers of \(f\). In the case of complete intersections, the result is however one unit weaker than 4.5.

blocks.title: Proposition 4.10.
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.10.

Under the general hypotheses of 4.5, suppose that

\(a\) \(x\) is an isolated point of non-smoothness in its fiber.

\(b\) For every point \(y\) of \(X_{\bar\eta}\), whose closure in \(X_{\bar\eta}\) is of dimension \(d(y)\), which is a generization of \(x\), we have \(\operatorname{prof}_{\mathrm{et},y}(X_{\bar\eta})\geq n-d(y)\), i.e. \[H^i_{\{y\}}(X_{\bar\eta})=0\quad\text{for }i<n-d(y).\]

\(c\) \(\operatorname{prof}_{\mathrm{et},x}(X_s)\geq n\).

Then, \(\phi^i_{\bar x}=0\) for \(i<n-1\).

Let us keep the notations of 4.5; we can apply 4.5 (1) with \(m=\infty\). By the local Lefschetz theorem (SGA 2 XIV), the hypothesis \(b\) provides \[H^i(V')\xrightarrow{\sim}H^i(V_s)\quad(i<n-1),\qquad H^i(V')\longleftarrow H^i(V_s)\quad(i=n-1),\] and we conclude by \(c\) that \(H^i(\bar V)=\varinjlim H^i(V')=0\) for \(i\leq n-1\), whence 4.10.
4.11. As in 4.8, we can give a variant of 4.10 which does not assume \(a\).

pages.title: 5. Action of the monodromy on the \(\pi_1\)

blocks.html: 5. Action of the monodromy on the \(\pi_1\)

In this § (= Grothendieck's exposé of 19/11/1968), we will make use of the semi-stable reduction theorem for curves. Artin and Winters [2] have given a direct proof of this theorem, which can also be deduced [3] from the analogous theorem for abelian varieties (IX § 3, using 3.6). We will also use the theory of Schlessinger (exposé VI, by Rim).
5.1. Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(C\) a projective curve over \(k\) and \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) a finite set of closed points of \(C\). We say that \(X=(C;x_1,\ldots,x_n)\) is stable if

1. \(a\) \(C\) is reduced and its only singularities are double points with distinct tangents;
  2. \(b\) the \(x_i\) are distinct, and are smooth points of \(C\);
  3. \(c\) if \(C_1\) is an irreducible component of \(C\), and \(E\) is the set of points of the normalization \(C'_1\) of \(C_1\) lying over a singular point of \(C_1\) or over one of the \(x_i\), then, if \(C'_1\) is of genus 0 (resp. 1), \(E\) has at least 3 (resp. 1) elements.
The condition \(c\) is equivalent to each of the following:

1. \(c'\) \(X\) has no nontrivial infinitesimal automorphisms.
  2. \(c''\) If \(\omega\) is the dualizing invertible sheaf, the invertible sheaf \(\omega'=\omega(\sum x_i)\) is ample.

An \(S\)-scheme \(C\), equipped with sections \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) is a stable curve over \(S\) if its geometric fibers are stable curves. In [3] §§ 1,2, only the case \(n=0\) is considered. The extension of the results of loc. cit. to the general case is trivial:

1. (1) For \(X/k\) stable, \(H^1(C,\omega'^{\otimes n})=0\) for \(n\geq2\) and \(\omega'^{\otimes n}\) is very ample for \(n\geq3\) (cf. [3] 1.2).
  2. (2) The formal moduli scheme \(M\) of \(X\) is smooth, and \(C\) remains singular above a divisor with normal crossings of \(M\).
  3. (3) For \(X\) and \(Y\) stable over \(S\), \(\operatorname{Isom}_S(X,Y)\) is finite and unramified over \(S\) (isomorphisms compatible with the marked points).
  4. (4) For \(X/k\) stable, \(\operatorname{Aut}_k(X)\to\operatorname{Aut}_k\operatorname{Pic}^0(C)\) is injective.
Finally we have

blocks.title: Proposition 5.1.1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1.1.

Let \(X_\eta=(C_\eta;x_1,\ldots,x_n)\) be stable over the generic point \(\eta\) of a trait \(S\), with \(C_\eta\) smooth. There exists a finite separable extension \(\eta'\) of \(\eta\) and a stable curve \(X'\) over the normalization \(S'\) of \(S\) in \(\eta'\) such that \[X'\otimes_{S'}\eta'=X_\eta\otimes_\eta\eta'.\]

This follows from the usual semi-stable reduction theorem, which allows us to take \(\eta'\) such that the geometric special fiber of the minimal model \(C''\) of \(C_{\eta'}\) over \(S'\) satisfies \(a\). We then blow up, iteratively if necessary, smooth points of \(C''\) so that \(b\) is satisfied. We then contract the chains of smooth rational components of self-intersection two containing no \(\bar x_i\) of the special fiber and obtain \(C'\) (cf. [3], proof of 2.3 p. 88).
One can check as in loc. cit. that \(C'\) exists as soon as the Jacobian of \(C_\eta\) has stable reduction. The smoothness hypothesis on \(C_\eta\) is moreover superfluous.

5.2. Let \(X_\eta\) be a geometrically connected scheme of finite type over the field of fractions of a strictly local trait \(S\). For \(\xi\) a geometric point of \(X_{\bar\eta}\) we have an exact sequence \[0\longrightarrow\pi_1(X_{\bar\eta},\xi)\longrightarrow\pi_1(X,\xi) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\longrightarrow0,\tag{5.2.1}\] whence a homomorphism from \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) into the group of outer automorphisms of \(\pi_1(X_{\bar\eta})\). Passing to the largest quotient prime to \(p\), \(\pi_1^{(p)}(X_{\bar\eta})\), of \(\pi_1(X_{\bar\eta})\), we find \[\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Aut}_{\mathrm{ext}}(\pi_1^{(p)}(X_{\bar\eta})).\tag{5.2.2}\]

Suppose that \(X_\eta\) has a rational point \(x\), and let \(\bar x\) be the geometric point \(\bar\eta\to\eta\xrightarrow{x}X_\eta\). For \(\xi=\bar x\), the exact sequence (5.2.2) splits canonically, whence \[[x]\colon\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Aut}(\pi_1(X_{\bar\eta},x))\longrightarrow \operatorname{Aut}(\pi_1^{(p)}(X_{\bar\eta},x)).\tag{5.2.3}\]

blocks.title: Theorem 5.3.
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.

The image of \(P\) under 5.2.2 (or 5.2.3, which amounts to the same thing) is finite.
5.3.1. Reduction to the case where \(X_\eta\) is geometrically normal and integral

Let \(X'_\eta\) be the normalization of \(X_\eta\), \((X'_{i\eta})_i\) the family of connected components of \(X'_\eta\), \(X''_\eta=X'_\eta\times_{X_\eta}X'_\eta\) and \((X''_{j\eta})_j\) the connected components of \(X''_\eta\). After passing if necessary to a finite extension of \(k(\eta)\), we may assume that the \(X'_{i\eta}\) are geometrically normal and integral, with a rational point \(x_i\), and that the \(X''_{j\eta}\) are geometrically connected, with a rational point \(y_j\). For \(k=1\) or 2 and \(p_k(y_j)\in X'_{i\eta}\), let us choose a class of paths \(\ell_{kij}\) from \(p_k(\bar y_j)\) to \(\bar x_i\): it is an element of a torsor under \(\pi_1(X'_{i\bar\eta},\bar x_i)\). Let \(\bar\ell_{kij}\) be the corresponding path "prime to \(p\)" (the same modulo \(\operatorname{Ker}(\pi\to\pi^{(p)})\)). We may take \(\bar\ell_{kij}\) invariant under \(P\): the set of paths prime to \(p\) is \(\varprojlim\) of finite sets of order prime to \(p\) on which the pro-\(p\)-group \(P\) acts.

Consequently, if a subgroup of finite index \(P'\) of \(P\) acts trivially on the \(\pi_1^{(p)}(X'_{i\bar\eta},\bar x_i)\), it follows from the Van Kampen formulas (i.e. from the fact that \(X'_\eta\to X_\eta\) is of effective descent for étale coverings) that \(P'\) acts trivially on \(\pi_1^{(p)}(X_{\bar\eta},\bar x)\).

5.3.2. For \(X_{\bar\eta}\) normal and \(U\) an open subset, \(\pi_1(U)\) maps onto \(\pi_1(X_{\bar\eta})\); we may therefore assume \(X_{\bar\eta}\) smooth and affine. Cutting by generic hyperplane sections (this replaces \(S\) by the strict localization of \(S\) at the generic point of the special fiber of an \(\mathbb A_S^n\)) and applying Bertini, we reduce to assuming that \(X_\eta\) is smooth, geometrically connected and of dimension one. After passing if necessary to a finite extension of \(k(\eta)\), we may assume that \(X_\eta\) is the complement in a smooth projective curve \(\bar X_\eta\) of a finite set of rational points \(x_i\), whose number we are free to increase. Applying the semi-stable reduction theorem (5.1.1), we reduce to assuming that there exists a stable curve \(X=(C;x_1,\ldots,x_n)\) over \(S\) such that \(X_\eta=C_\eta-\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}\). We then apply the following result:

blocks.title: Theorem 5.4.
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.4.

Let \(S\) be a strictly local scheme, \(f\colon\bar X\to S\) a proper flat morphism, whose special fiber is a curve whose only points of non-smoothness are ordinary double points and \(Y\) a subscheme of \(\bar X\), a union of a finite number of disjoint sections contained in the smoothness locus. Let \(x\) be a rational point of \(X=\bar X-Y\). Let \(U\) be the open subset of \(S\) above which \(X\) is smooth, and \(\xi\) a geometric point of \(U\). Then the image of \(\pi_1(U,\xi)\) in \(\operatorname{Aut}(\pi_1^{(p)}(X_\xi,x\xi))\) is abelian and prime to \(p\).
One should beware that we can make \(\pi_1(U,\xi)\) act on \(\pi_1^{(p)}(X_\xi,x\xi)\) only because the \(\pi_1^{(p)}\) satisfy the specialization theorem.

We reduce to the case \(S\) complete, then to the universal case where \(S\) is a formal moduli scheme for the special fiber, equipped with its sections. \(S\) is then a ring of formal power series over a Cohen ring: \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(W[[t_1,\ldots,t_n]])\) and \(U\) is the complement of a divisor with normal crossings with equation \(t_1\cdots t_m=0\). In this universal case, by Abhyankar's lemma, \(\pi_1(U,\xi)\) is abelian and prime to \(p\), whence the theorem.
Remark 5.5. One can prove a result analogous to 5.4 in arbitrary relative dimension by considering a generic plane section of dimension one and applying Bertini.

pages.title: 6. Appendix: arithmetic proof of the stable reduction theorem (by P. Deligne)

blocks.html: 6. Appendix: arithmetic proof of the stable reduction theorem (by P. Deligne)

Let \(S\) be a trait and \(A_\eta\) an abelian variety over \(k(\eta)\), of dimension \(d\ne0\). For \(S'\to S\) a local morphism of traits, we denote by \(A_{\eta'}\) the abelian variety over \(k(\eta')\) deduced from \(A_\eta\) by extension of scalars. Let \(\mathcal A_{S'}\) be the Néron model of \(A_{\eta'}\) over \(S'\), \(\mathcal A_{S',s'}\) its special fiber and \(\mathcal A^0_{S',s'}\) the identity component of \(\mathcal A_{S',s'}\). The stable reduction theorem is the following.

blocks.title: Theorem 6.1.
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.1.

For suitable \(S'\), \(A_{\eta'}\) has stable reduction, i.e. \(\mathcal A^0_{S',s'}\) is an extension of an abelian variety by a torus.

It follows rather easily from 6.1 that we may take \(S'\) such that \(k(\eta')\) is a finite separable extension of \(k(\eta)\). Suppose first that the residue field of \(S\) is of finite type over the prime field (the same argument would work for \(k(s)\) purely inseparable over such a field).

Let \(\ell\) be a prime number prime to the residual characteristic exponent \(p\), \(T_\ell(A)\) the Tate module, \(V_\ell(A)=T_\ell(A)\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell\) and \(\rho\colon\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\to\operatorname{GL}(T_\ell(A))\) the natural representation. A preliminary extension of scalars reduces us to the case where the inertia group \(I\) acts unipotently (1.2), hence through its largest pro-\(\ell\)-group quotient \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\) (0.3). Let \(T\) be the image in \(\operatorname{GL}(T_\ell(A))\) of a generator of \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\) and \(r\) the largest integer \(\geq0\) such that \((T-1)^r\ne0\). We have:

blocks.title: Lemma 6.2.
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2.

The map \[\sigma\in I,\ v\in V_\ell(A)\longmapsto(\rho(\sigma)-1)^r(v)\] induces a morphism and an isomorphism

\[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ V_\ell(A)_I\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell(r)\ar[r]^M\ar[d]&V_\ell(A)^I\\ V_\ell(A)/\operatorname{Ker}(T-1)^r\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell(r)\ar[r]^{\sim}& \operatorname{Im}(T-1)^r\ar[u] }\tag{6.2.1}\] \(V_\ell(A)_I\) and \(V_\ell(A)^I\) are \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar s/s)\)-modules and \(M\) is Galois-equivariant.
6.3. We say that an \(\ell\)-adic representation \(\tau\colon\operatorname{Gal}(\bar s/s)\to\operatorname{GL}(V)\) is of weight \(n\) (\(n\in\mathbb Z\)) if the following condition is satisfied:

(\(\star\)) For a suitable model \(X\) of \(k(s)\) (\(X\) = an irreducible variety of finite type over the prime field, with \(k(s)=k(X)\)), \(\tau\) factors through \(\pi_1(X,\bar s)\) and for \(x\) a closed point of \(X\), the eigenvalues of \(\tau(F_x)\) are algebraic numbers whose complex conjugates are all of absolute value \(|k(x)|^{n/2}\) (\(F_x\) denotes the geometric Frobenius \(\phi_x^{-1}\)).
If \(V\) (resp. \(W\)) is of weight \(n\) (resp. \(m\)) and \(n\ne m\), we have \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{Gal}}(V,W)=0\).

6.4. It follows from the universal property of the Néron model that \(V_\ell(A)^I=V_\ell(\mathcal A^0_{S,s})\). If \(a\) is the dimension of the largest abelian quotient of \(\mathcal A^0_{S,s}\) and \(m\) the dimension of the multiplicative part of \(\mathcal A^0_{S,s}\), then by Weil, \(V_\ell(A)^I\) is thus an extension of a representation of dimension \(2a\) and weight \(-1\) by a representation of dimension \(m\) and weight \(-2\).

6.5. Let \(A^*\) be the dual abelian variety of \(A\). Recall that \(V_\ell(A^*)\) and \(V_\ell(A)\) (hence \(V_\ell(A^*)^I\) and \(V_\ell(A)_I\)) are in duality with values in \(\mathbb Q_\ell(1)\). If \(a^*\) and \(m^*\) are defined for \(A^*\) as \(a\) and \(m\) are for \(A\), it follows from 6.4 that \(V_\ell(A)_I\) is an extension of a representation of dimension \(m^*\) and of weight 0 by a representation of dimension \(2a^*\) and of weight \(-1\).

6.6. The Galois module \(\mathbb Q_\ell(r)\) is of weight \(-2r\). Since \(\operatorname{Im}(T-1)^r\ne0\), we deduce from 6.4, 6.5 and the isomorphism 6.2 that \(r\leq1\). If \(r=0\), we have \[\begin{cases}a+m\leq d,\\2a+m=2d,\end{cases}\] hence \(m=0\), \(a=d\) and \(A_\eta\) has good reduction. Suppose that \(r=1\). We then deduce from 6.2 that \(\operatorname{Im}(T-1)\) is of weight \(-2\) and that \(V_\ell(A)/V_\ell(A)^I\) is of weight 0; these spaces have the same dimension \(m_1\leq m\).

We then have \[\dim V_\ell(A)^I=2d-m_1=2a+m,\] \[2\dim\mathcal A_{S,s}\geq2(a+m)\geq2a+m+m_1=2d=2\dim A_{S,s},\] so that \(A_\eta\) has stable reduction (\(\dim\mathcal A_{S,s}=a+m\)). We have obtained at the same time that \(m_1=m\), i.e. that \(\operatorname{Im}(T-1)\subset V_\ell(A)^I\simeq V_\ell(\mathcal A^0_{S,s})\) is the \(V_\ell\) of the multiplicative part of \(\mathcal A^0_{S,s}\).
6.7. To treat the general case, we will use (0.5) (cf. 1.3). We reduce to assuming that

1. \(a\) the conditions of (0.5.2) or (0.5.3) are satisfied;
  2. \(b\) \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar\eta/\eta)\) acts trivially on \(A_\ell\);
  3. \(c\) the action of \(I\) on \(T_\ell(A)\) is unipotent.

Under these hypotheses, we will prove that \(A_\eta\) has stable reduction. The hypothesis \(a\) allows us to apply (0.5.1) to obtain \(S=\varprojlim S_i\) as in 1.3, whose notations we resume. Let \(s_i\) be the closed point of \(S_i\).

For \(i\) large enough, the identity component \(\mathcal A^0\) of the Néron model \(\mathcal A_S\) comes from a smooth group scheme with connected fibers \(\mathcal A_i\) over \(S_i\), and \((\mathcal A_i)_\ell\) is constant on \(S_i-D_i\). The group \(\pi_1(S_i-D_i,\bar\eta)\) acts on \(T_\ell(A)\), and acts trivially on \(T_\ell(A)/\ell T_\ell(A)\simeq A_\ell\). As in 1.3, we see that \(P_i\) acts trivially. We then have by (1.3.1): \[T_\ell(A)_I=T_\ell(A)_{I_i},\qquad T_\ell(\mathcal A_{i,s_i})=T_\ell(\mathcal A^0_s)=T_\ell(A)^I=T_\ell(A^{I_i}),\] and (6.2.1) is a diagram of \(\operatorname{Gal}(\bar s_i/s_i)\)-modules to which we can apply the arguments 6.4 to 6.6.
